steps.title: Look for an invasive advantage
steps.content: Keller and Stein concluded that invasives are advantaged by delays in the onset of subfreezing temperatures in Alaska. A supporting finding must therefore show more than both plant types responding to a warm autumn. It must show that the invasive species, P. aviculare, benefits more from the delay than the native species, V. edule.

steps.title: Reject weak findings
steps.content: Choice B says neither species showed significant variation in when leaf production stopped, which would not support any advantage from delayed freezing. Choice C says P. aviculare stopped producing leaves sooner than V. edule in delayed-freezing years, pointing away from an invasive advantage. Choice D adds early spring onset, a confounding detail that weakens the link to autumn delays.

steps.title: Compare the autumn extension
steps.content: Choice A says both species produced leaves later into autumn in years with late subfreezing temperature onset, but that the extension was much greater for P. aviculare than for V. edule. That directly shows the invasive gaining more from the delayed freeze. The answer is A.
